objections.text: Our current Workday integration does not support the custom payroll attributes that Lumos HR requires. We would need significant API work on our end.
At 750k ARR, this represents a 12 percent increase to our software spend. We need to see measurable ROI within the first 18 months to justify the investment to our board.
We have concerns about data security and compliance. Your SOC 2 certification is Type I, not Type II. We need Type II for our procurement to move forward.
The implementation timeline of 90 days is too aggressive. Our IT team is already stretched thin with the new ERP migration.
Our procurement team requires three vendor references from companies in similar industries with at least 1000 employees. We were unable to obtain those references.
How does your platform handle our custom leave accrual policies that vary by employee classification and tenure? Your documentation seems to address only standard policies.

channel.name: deal-talent-compass
channel.topic: Talent Compass Solutions - HR Tech - $750k ARR

messages.body: Just wrapped discovery with Talent Compass. Jennifer Walsh and Amanda Foster are serious about leaving Workday's leave management behind. Custom accrual policies are killing them.
How messy is the Workday integration picture? That's where these deals usually break.
Really messy. They need custom payroll attributes synced back to Workday. Not standard API stuff. David Patel from IT said it could be 2 to 3 weeks of internal effort just to scope it.
Got it. Can we do a technical deep-dive before demo? Sarah should probably lead that so we can quantify the integration scope.
Already scheduled Sarah for April 8. Demo is locked in for that same day with Amanda and a few others.
Demo wrapped. Bad news: Robert Martinez is worried about ROI. At 750k a year, that's a 12 percent bump to their software spend. He needs measurable returns in 18 months or his board kills it.
That is their biggest concern or just one of them?

One of three. David Patel also confirmed the Workday API integration is going to chew up 2 to 3 weeks of his team. And they want references from similar-sized companies before they even think about moving forward.
Did Amanda say the leave module covers their tiered accrual policies? That was the whole reason they wanted us.
Yes. Amanda confirmed it handles all their classifications and tenure-based policies. That part is actually solved. Problem is everything else.

subject: Exploring HR Tech Integration Solutions for Talent Compass
body:
Jennifer, I was reviewing Talent Compass Solutions recent expansion and noticed your team has been scaling HR operations significantly across your 500-1200 person organization. With Workday and Salesforce running your core systems, I suspect you're dealing with some integration friction that's creating manual work for your payroll and people operations teams.

We work specifically with mid-market HR services firms like yours where custom payroll attributes and complex leave policies need to sync seamlessly across your tech stack. Most teams we talk to are surprised how much time they're spending on data reconciliation between systems that should just talk to each other.

I'd love to spend 20 minutes understanding how your team currently handles payroll data flows and compliance reporting. No pitch, just a genuine conversation about where the gaps might be.

Would next Tuesday or Wednesday work for a quick call?

Best,
Marcus

note_preview: Jennifer Walsh confirmed as champion, strong HR modernization vision
author: Marcus Chen
content: Jennifer Walsh is locked in as our champion and deeply aligned on the HR modernization vision. She's frustrated with current Workday limitations and sees Lumos as a path forward. Strong starting position but need to get IT and Finance aligned before we hit Demo.

subject: Re: Exploring HR Tech Integration Solutions for Talent Compass
body: Marcus, thanks for reaching out about Lumos HR. I appreciate you taking the time to understand our situation. Before we move forward, I need to be direct about some concerns my team has raised. We're currently pretty deeply embedded in Workday, and our custom payroll attributes are fairly complex. I want to make sure there won't be significant integration friction on your end before we invest time in a deeper conversation. Also, our IT director David has flagged questions about how your platform handles our specific leave accrual policies - we have different rules by employee classification and tenure, and the documentation we reviewed seemed to focus on standard scenarios. Can you speak to how you'd approach those customizations? I'm genuinely interested in modernizing our HR tech stack, but I need to know we're solving real problems, not creating new ones. When you have a moment, could you address these specific points so we can determine if this is worth scheduling a broader conversation with the team?

subject: Discovery Call Scheduled - March 23 at 2 PM EST
body:
Hi Jennifer, Thanks for the thoughtful conversation last week about Talent Compass's HR modernization roadmap. I really appreciated your perspective on the gaps you're seeing between Workday and your custom payroll workflows. I think there's genuine potential here to streamline your processes without the heavy lift you've been managing.

I'd like to bring Sarah Kowalski, our Sales Engineer, into the mix for a deeper technical dive. She works directly with companies your size on integration strategy and can speak to how we handle the custom attributes and compliance requirements that matter most to your operation.

Would March 23 at 2 PM EST work for a 45-minute discovery call? We can dial in from our end, and I'm happy to work around whatever schedule suits your team best.

Looking forward to it.

Best,
Marcus

subject: Re: Discovery Call Scheduled - March 23 at 2 PM EST
body: Hi Marcus, Thanks so much for scheduling this. I've been looking at your platform overview and I'm genuinely impressed by how you handle custom payroll attributes - that's been a real pain point for us with our current Workday setup. A few things I'd love to dig into on Tuesday: how your API integrations work with our existing tech stack, and whether you've worked with other mid-market HR services firms on similar implementations. I've also flagged this with David from IT and Robert from Finance so they're aware we're exploring options. One quick question before we talk - do you have any case studies or examples from companies in our size range that have gone through the onboarding process? Just want to set realistic expectations on our end. Really looking forward to the conversation. See you at 2 PM EST on the 23rd. Jennifer

title: Discovery Call - HR Tech Integration Requirements
transcript:
Marcus Chen: Good morning Jennifer, Amanda. Thanks for taking the time today. I know you're both busy, so I want to make sure we focus on what matters most for Talent Compass. Jennifer, when we last spoke, you mentioned modernizing your HR tech stack. Can you walk me through what's driving that initiative right now?

Jennifer Walsh: Absolutely. We've been on Workday for about five years now, and it's solid for core HR and payroll. But our processes around leave management, custom accrual policies, and reporting have become increasingly manual. Amanda's team is spending way too much time on spreadsheet reconciliation that should be automated.

Amanda Foster: That's exactly right. We have really complex leave policies because we manage employees across different classifications - full-time, contractors, international staff - and tenure matters. Workday handles the basics, but our custom rules require constant manual intervention. It's a compliance risk and a drain on resources.

Marcus Chen: That's really helpful context. So you need something that can handle your custom leave accrual logic at scale. Before we dig into what Lumos HR can do, let me ask - when you think about bringing a new system into your tech environment, what are the biggest concerns?

Jennifer Walsh: Integration is number one for us. Workday is the system of record, and we cannot afford disconnected data or manual syncing between platforms. We also need to make sure any new tool doesn't disrupt payroll processing. That's mission-critical.

Amanda Foster: And from an operations perspective, I need to know that the implementation won't require Amanda's team to basically stop everything for three months. We're already stretched thin, and I'm honestly concerned about whether we even have the bandwidth to manage a major system integration.

Marcus Chen: Those are really fair concerns. Let me address the Workday integration piece first. Lumos HR has bidirectional API integration with Workday that handles employee data, compensation, and leave balances. The key question for us is understanding your custom payroll attributes - do you push custom fields back into Workday, or are those only in your downstream systems?

Amanda Foster: We have some custom attributes in Workday - things like leave type classifications and accrual rates by tenure band. Those feed into our payroll system downstream. So whatever you implement needs to sync cleanly in both directions.

Marcus Chen: That's the kind of detail we need to validate. I'm going to loop in Sarah, our Sales Engineer, because she'll need to review your Workday configuration to confirm we can handle those custom attributes. Jennifer, on the timeline concern - what would feel realistic for implementation? Are we thinking phased rollout, or does it need to happen in one go?

Jennifer Walsh: We'd prefer phased if possible. Maybe pilot with one employee group first, then scale. That gives us breathing room and lets us validate the leave accrual logic before full deployment.

Amanda Foster: That would help us tremendously. But I still need to understand what the resource lift is on our end. Training, data validation, testing - how much of that falls on my team?

Marcus Chen: Sarah can walk through that in detail, but typically we handle the heavy lifting on configuration and integration. Your team would focus on testing against your policies and business rules, which is where your expertise is critical. We'll schedule a technical deep-dive with Sarah and David Patel from IT so we can scope everything accurately.

Jennifer Walsh: That sounds reasonable. I want to move quickly here - HR modernization is a board priority for us this year. What's the next step?

Marcus Chen: Sarah will reach out by end of week to schedule a two-hour technical session with you and David. She'll need your Workday admin access details beforehand so she can review the custom fields. We'll come back with a detailed integration assessment and a realistic implementation timeline. Does that work?

Jennifer Walsh: Perfect. Amanda, does that timeline work for you?

Amanda Foster: Yes, I'll coordinate with IT. But I want to be clear - this only moves forward if we're confident on the resource question.

Marcus Chen: Understood. That's exactly what the technical session will clarify.
summary: Jennifer Walsh and Amanda Foster outlined their need to modernize leave management and accrual policy automation beyond Workday's native capabilities. Key concerns surfaced around Workday integration complexity with custom payroll attributes, resource constraints for implementation, and phased rollout requirements. Marcus Chen committed to having Sarah Kowalski conduct a detailed technical assessment of the Workday integration and resource requirements.
objections_raised: Our custom Workday payroll attributes and leave classification fields must sync bidirectionally - need to validate technical feasibility
We lack bandwidth for implementation - team is already stretched thin and cannot afford significant resource commitment
Prefer phased rollout approach over aggressive single-wave implementation
next_steps: Sarah Kowalski to schedule two-hour technical deep-dive with Jennifer Walsh, Amanda Foster, and David Patel from IT by end of week 2026-03-28
Obtain Workday admin access details from Amanda Foster to review custom payroll attributes and leave accrual fields
Deliver detailed integration assessment and realistic phased implementation timeline within one week of technical review

note_preview: David Patel raised Workday integration API complexity concern today
author: Marcus Chen
content: David Patel flagged a major concern about our Workday integration today - their custom payroll attributes aren't supported by our standard API, and they'll need significant work on their end to make it happen. This is going to be a technical blocker if we can't show a cleaner integration path. Need to loop in Sarah to assess whether we can offer any custom development or if this stays a deal risk.

note_preview: Objection logged - Workday custom payroll attributes not supported by Lumos
author: Marcus Chen
content: David Patel flagged that their Workday instance has custom payroll attributes we don't natively support and they'd need to do significant API work on their end to make it work. This is a legitimate technical blocker we need to assess with Sarah before we go further with the demo.

subject: Follow-up: Integration Requirements and Next Steps
body:
Jennifer and David, thanks for the thoughtful discussion yesterday. I wanted to follow up on the Workday integration question that came up - specifically around those custom payroll attributes. I can see why that's a critical concern given your current setup.

We've worked with several mid-market HR services companies navigating similar Workday configurations. The API complexity you mentioned isn't uncommon, but it's also not insurmountable. Rather than make assumptions, I'd like to dig deeper into your specific payroll attribute structure. If you're open to it, I'd suggest a brief technical deep-dive call with David and our solutions architect Sarah to map out what a phased API integration approach could look like.

The goal isn't to oversell - it's to give you accurate visibility into what we'd actually need on both sides to make this work. If it turns out the effort required doesn't align with your timeline, better to know that now.

Would mid-week next week work to schedule that 30-minute technical call? Happy to work around your calendar.

Best,
Sarah

subject: Re: Follow-up: Integration Requirements and Next Steps
body:
Sarah, I appreciate you taking the time to dive into this with us. You're right that the Workday integration complexity is a real constraint on our end - we've got some fairly non-standard payroll attributes tied to our classification and tenure structures that our previous vendor couldn't handle cleanly.

Before we commit time to a technical deep-dive, I need to be honest about where we stand. Our IT capacity is stretched thin right now, and I'm concerned about the resource commitment a phased API approach would require from our team. We're already managing the new ERP migration, and adding another integration project could create bandwidth issues.

That said, I don't want to dismiss this outright. If you can give me a realistic sense upfront of the effort required on our side - hours, complexity level, timeline - I can determine if this is even feasible for us in the next 12 months. A 30-minute call makes sense only if we've got clarity on that scope first.

Can you outline the resource requirements before we schedule?

subject: Demo Resources and Technical Documentation
body: Hi David and Amanda, Following up on our discovery call last week, I wanted to send over some resources that directly address the Workday integration questions David raised. I have attached our technical documentation on custom payroll attribute mapping, along with a case study from another mid-market HR Services company that successfully integrated similar custom leave accrual logic. I also wanted to clarify something important: while our standard Workday connector handles most attribute types, we do support custom fields through our API extension framework. This means the payroll attributes you mentioned should be workable without requiring extensive development on your end. Rather than making assumptions, I think it would be valuable to do a focused technical deep-dive with you and David before our full demo. This way we can walk through your specific Workday configuration and confirm the integration approach upfront. Would you have 45 minutes this week for a quick technical sync? Marcus and I are flexible on timing. Looking forward to moving this forward.

title: Product Demo - Payroll and Leave Management
transcript:
Marcus Chen: Good afternoon, everyone. Thanks for taking the time today. We're excited to walk through the Lumos HR platform and show you how it can streamline your payroll and leave management processes. Jennifer, David, Amanda - we know you're evaluating solutions that integrate seamlessly with Workday, so we've built this demo around your tech stack. Let's start with the payroll module. Sarah, can you take us through the dashboard?

Sarah Kowalski: Absolutely. Here's our core payroll interface. You can see real-time integration with Workday data feeds. We sync employee records, compensation changes, and tax withholdings automatically. For Talent Compass, this means your payroll team spends less time on manual data entry and reconciliation.

Amanda Foster: That's helpful. But I need to understand how you handle our custom payroll attributes. We have several non-standard deduction codes tied to our benefits structure - some are employee-specific, others are project-based. Will your system support that level of customization?

Sarah Kowalski: That's a great question. We support standard payroll attributes out of the box, and we can configure additional fields through our setup process. However, for truly custom attributes that require API-level integration with Workday, that typically requires some development work on both sides.

David Patel: That's exactly what concerns me. We went through a major Workday implementation two years ago. Any integration that requires custom API development is a resource drain for my team. We're already stretched thin. What level of API work are we talking about here?

Marcus Chen: David, that's a fair concern. Let me be direct - for complex custom payroll attributes, yes, there would likely be some API work required. We'd need to map your custom fields to our system. But we have a certified partner network that can handle that integration work, so it doesn't have to fall entirely on your IT team.

David Patel: How much does that partner work typically cost, and how long does it take?

Marcus Chen: That depends on the scope. For moderate customization, we're typically talking three to six weeks and anywhere from fifteen to thirty thousand dollars. But let's not get ahead of ourselves - let's see exactly what you need. Sarah, can you show the leave management capabilities?

Sarah Kowalski: Of course. This is our leave accrual and tracking module. You can set up unlimited leave policies with rules based on tenure, employee classification, geography - whatever you need. We handle carryover, payout, and compliance reporting for all fifty states.

Amanda Foster: How does the system handle our tiered leave policies? We have different accrual rates for full-time, part-time, and contractors, and accrual accelerates after three and five years of tenure.

Sarah Kowalski: Perfect use case for our system. I can show you exactly how to configure those tiers. You'd set up policy groups by employee classification and tenure bands. The system calculates accruals automatically on each pay cycle.

Jennifer Walsh: This is encouraging. My team has been managing this through spreadsheets and manual calculations. How long would implementation take, and what's the training commitment?

Marcus Chen: Standard implementation is ninety days from contract signature. That includes data migration, configuration, testing, and training for your key users. We'd schedule weekly check-ins with your team.

David Patel: Ninety days might be aggressive for us given current IT capacity, but let's see where this goes. I do want to circle back on that Workday API piece. That's a real concern for us.

Jennifer Walsh: I agree we need clarity on that integration risk. But Amanda, does the leave functionality address what you've been struggling with?

Amanda Foster: Yes, actually. If we can confirm the custom payroll integration won't break our current Workday setup, this could be valuable.

Marcus Chen: Let's do this - we'll send over detailed technical documentation on our Workday integration approach and get Sarah's team together with David to map out your custom payroll attributes. That way we can give you a clear picture of what API work, if any, is actually needed.

David Patel: That makes sense. I want to see the specific integration requirements before we move forward.

Jennifer Walsh: Agreed. Can you send that over this week?

Marcus Chen: Absolutely. We'll have Sarah reach out to David tomorrow to schedule a technical deep-dive. Jennifer, does that work for moving forward?

Jennifer Walsh: Yes. Once we have clarity on the integration piece, we can loop in Robert and our procurement team for the commercial discussion.
summary: Lumos HR presented payroll and leave management capabilities to Talent Compass stakeholders. Amanda Foster confirmed the leave module addresses their tiered policy needs, but David Patel raised a significant concern about custom payroll attribute integration with Workday requiring API development work. The team agreed to schedule a technical deep-dive to assess integration requirements and scope before advancing to commercial discussions.
objections_raised: Our custom payroll attributes require API-level integration with Workday - how much development work is required on our end?
How do you handle our tiered leave policies with acceleration based on tenure and employee classification?
The ninety-day implementation timeline may be aggressive given IT team capacity constraints
next_steps: Sarah Kowalski to send detailed Workday integration technical documentation to David Patel this week
Schedule technical deep-dive between Sarah Kowalski and David Patel to map Talent Compass custom payroll attributes and assess API requirements
Once technical integration clarity achieved, escalate to Robert Martinez CFO and procurement team for commercial evaluation

title: Deal Review — Talent Compass Solutions
transcript: Jennifer (Manager): Great news on the demo call with Talent Compass. Marcus, walk us through what happened. They came in concerned about integration complexity, correct? Marcus (AE): Exactly. Before the demo, they were worried about how our platform would integrate with their existing Workday instance. But Sarah knocked it out of the park with the demo today. The payroll and leave management walkthrough really addressed their technical concerns. Sarah (SE): Thanks, Marcus. I think the key was showing them the pre-built connectors and the actual integration timeline. Once they saw we could handle their data migration in phases, their body language completely changed. Jennifer (Manager): So the concern shifted to neutral. That's a positive trajectory. Marcus (AE): Definitely. They asked great technical questions in the second half, which shows they're now thinking about implementation rather than feasibility. Sarah (SE): I also demonstrated our leave management module, which solved a pain point they mentioned in discovery. The CFO seemed particularly interested in the audit trail capabilities. Jennifer (Manager): Excellent. What's the next step? Marcus (AE): We've scheduled the security and compliance review for April 28th. They want to do a deep dive on data security, SOC 2 compliance, and how we handle PII. I'm not seeing any red flags yet. Sarah (SE): I'll prepare a comprehensive security whitepaper and get our compliance officer to join that call. Jennifer (Manager): Good. Keep the momentum going. This deal is looking strong. Let's make sure we're over-prepared for the compliance discussion since that's often where deals slow down. Marcus (AE): Will do. I'll also check in with their IT director early next week to understand any specific compliance requirements they might have.
summary: Post-demo deal review confirming positive sentiment shift from concerned to neutral. Talent Compass responded well to payroll and leave management demonstrations, and technical integration concerns were adequately addressed. Team is prepared for upcoming security and compliance review on April 28th.
action_items: Sarah to prepare comprehensive security and compliance whitepaper for April 28th call
Marcus to schedule pre-call conversation with Talent Compass IT director to understand specific compliance requirements
Confirm compliance officer availability for April 28th security and compliance review call

note_preview: Product demo completed, Amanda Foster raised custom leave accrual questions
author: Marcus Chen
content: Demo went smoothly with Amanda Foster today. She zeroed in on our custom leave accrual handling and whether we support their employee classification and tenure-based policies. Sarah walked through the standard policy configurations but Amanda seemed skeptical about the flexibility - this is a gap we need to close before evaluation stage.

subject: Re: Demo Resources and Technical Documentation
body: Sarah, thanks for sending over those resources. I reviewed the technical documentation and the case study was helpful context. David and I have been discussing the Workday integration piece, and I have to be honest - we're still not entirely confident about the feasibility without seeing it in action against our actual environment. During the demo yesterday, when we walked through our custom leave accrual policies by employee classification and tenure, I noticed the standard workflow examples didn't quite align with how we've set things up. The API extension framework you mentioned sounds promising, but we need specifics on effort and timeline from our IT perspective before we can move forward. I'm concerned about committing to an integration approach that could require unexpected development work on our end. Would it be possible to schedule that technical sync with David as soon as possible? He'll want to review the specific API requirements and confirm resource availability. He's generally available Thursday or Friday afternoon if either of those work for you and Marcus. Looking forward to clarifying this before we proceed.

note_preview: Objection raised - custom leave accrual policies by tenure classification
author: Marcus Chen
content: Amanda Foster raised a solid question about how we handle their custom leave accrual that varies by employee classification and tenure. Sarah walked through the policy engine documentation but Amanda wasn't convinced our standard templates would cover their scenario without significant customization. Need to dig into their exact policy matrix and figure out if this is a configuration issue or a real gap in our platform.

subject: Custom Leave Accrual Policy - Clarification and Next Steps
body: Hi Amanda, Thanks for your thoughtful questions during the demo on April 8th regarding custom leave accrual policies. I wanted to follow up on your concern about handling classifications and tenure-based variations. I reviewed our documentation with our product team, and I realize it focused primarily on standard accrual models. Your leave policies are more nuanced than our initial materials suggested. Here's what we can do: I'd like to schedule a technical deep-dive with you and David Patel to map your specific accrual logic against our platform capabilities. We have customers with similar tenure-based structures, and our API does support custom attributes. The question is whether those customizations align with your current Workday setup. Rather than making assumptions, let's get the details right upfront. I have time this week or early next week. Which works better for your team? I'll prepare a technical requirements document beforehand so we can be efficient with everyone's time. Looking forward to clarifying this. Best, Sarah

note_preview: Custom leave accrual objection resolved, Amanda satisfied with solution approach
author: Marcus Chen
content: Amanda Foster walked through her custom leave accrual scenarios with Sarah during the demo and confirmed our platform can handle the tenure-based and classification-dependent policies they need. This was a big win since it was her main concern coming in. She's now bought in on the technical fit and said she'd recommend moving forward to Jennifer.

subject: Re: Custom Leave Accrual Policy - Clarification and Next Steps
body: Hi Sarah, Thanks so much for diving into this so quickly. I really appreciate you looping in your product team to understand our nuances around tenure-based accrual. You're right that our policies are more complex than standard models - we have three employee classifications with different accrual rates based on tenure thresholds, and it's been a pain point in our current system. I'm excited about the possibility of mapping this properly into Lumos HR. A technical deep-dive makes sense. David and I are both available next week - Tuesday or Wednesday afternoon would work well for us. Having that requirements document prepared beforehand is perfect. We want to make sure we're not overlooking any gaps with the Workday integration either, so if you can touch on API considerations for custom attributes, that would be really helpful. This was exactly the kind of collaborative approach we were hoping for. Looking forward to the discussion. Best, Amanda

subject: Security Compliance Review - SOC 2 Certification Status
body: David, following up on our demo last week and the technical discussions with your team. I wanted to address the security compliance piece directly since I know that's a critical gating factor for your procurement process. We currently hold SOC 2 Type I certification, which I understand may not align with your internal requirements. I want to be transparent about our roadmap here. We're actively working toward Type II certification and expect completion by Q3 2026. In the interim, I'd like to schedule a detailed security review call with you to walk through our control environment, data handling practices, and compliance posture. This will give your team concrete visibility into how we manage sensitive payroll and employee data, regardless of certification status. I've also asked Sarah to pull together our latest penetration test results and data residency documentation. Would you have 30 minutes next week to discuss? I think it's important we address your security concerns head-on so there are no surprises as we move forward with Talent Compass. Let me know what works for your calendar. Best, Marcus

subject: Re: Security Compliance Review - SOC 2 Certification Status
body: Marcus, thanks for reaching out on the security review. I appreciate you raising this directly with our procurement team in mind. I need to be candid about where we stand on SOC 2 certification. Our current Type I certification covers the audit of our controls as of a specific point in time, but I understand your team requires Type II, which demonstrates operating effectiveness over a minimum six-month period. We are actively working toward Type II certification and expect to complete the assessment by Q3 2026. I recognize this timeline may not align with your evaluation window. Given the sensitivity around data security and compliance for your HR operations, I want to discuss whether there are interim controls or attestations we could provide to bridge this gap. Our security posture is robust, and we have successfully implemented payroll data protection protocols for similar clients. Would you be open to a detailed security controls walk-through with your IT team this week? Sarah can coordinate technical documentation and answer any specific questions about our infrastructure. I want to ensure we address your compliance concerns properly rather than rush past legitimate security questions.

messages.body: Talent Compass call today surfaced three hard blockers. Escalating because I think we need help on security and legal side.
What are we looking at?

They need SOC 2 Type II but we only have Type I. Their procurement won't move without Type II. Also their IT team is already slammed and 90 days is not realistic for implementation. And API integration with Workday is not straightforward.
Type II timeline is what, 6 months out? We can talk through a compliance roadmap but they need to understand the reality of certification.
I told them we would work with their security team on a compliance roadmap. Can you set that up? Also offered phased implementation to ease the 90-day pressure and some cost-sharing on the API work.
How did they react to the cost-sharing offer?
Lukewarm. David Patel wants to know what we can actually absorb. And Robert is still hung up on the 18-month ROI requirement before he takes it to the board.
We need references. Where are we on that? They asked for vendor refs from similar-size companies.
I committed to getting three refs from companies with at least 1000 employees in similar industries. That is proving harder than I thought. Most of our wins are smaller or different verticals.
I can check our customer base. We might have two that fit. But agreed, finding three similar-size HR tech wins is tough. Want me to pull a list?

title: Security and Compliance Review
transcript: Marcus Chen: Good morning David, Robert. Thanks for taking the time. I know you both have concerns about our security and compliance posture, so I wanted to walk through our certifications and answer any questions head-on. David, I'll start with you since IT infrastructure is your domain. Marcus Chen: We currently hold SOC 2 Type I certification. I understand from your previous emails that you may need Type II. Can you walk me through what that means for your procurement process? David Patel: Yeah, that's the core issue here. Type I is a point-in-time audit. Type II requires a minimum of six months of operational testing and demonstrates controls over a period of time. Our security team is very specific about this. Given that you're handling payroll data and integrating with Workday, they want that ongoing verification. It's not just a checkbox. Marcus Chen: I appreciate that context. We're actively pursuing Type II certification right now. We should have it completed by Q4. Robert Martinez: Q4 is five months away. We need this resolved sooner if we're moving forward. Our board is already questioning the timeline and the investment size. Can you help me understand the risk profile here? What happens if we proceed without Type II in place? Marcus Chen: That's a fair question. Operationally, we can still integrate and the system performs identically. However, I understand your procurement requirements, and I respect that. The real question is whether we can work with your security and legal teams to document a path to Type II compliance. We could also discuss a phased implementation that reduces initial scope while we complete certification. David Patel: There's another layer to this. Our Workday integration doesn't support your custom payroll attributes out of the box. Your API documentation mentions standard attributes, but we have specific requirements for our shift-based workforce. What kind of customization work are we talking about on our end? Marcus Chen: That's important. Yes, we have standard payroll attributes that work for most organizations, but custom attributes require some configuration. Our professional services team typically handles that, but given your Workday architecture, there may be some API work required on your side. I'll be honest, it's not trivial. We're looking at probably two to three weeks of effort from your team. David Patel: Two to three weeks? My team is already stretched thin. We're in the middle of an ERP migration right now. That's not happening in parallel. Robert Martinez: So we're looking at security certifications we don't have, integration work that requires pulling my IT director's team away from other priorities, and we're still at 750k a year in new spend. Marcus, I need to see measurable ROI within the first eighteen months to justify this to the board. Do you have reference customers in our vertical with similar headcount that have realized that ROI? Marcus Chen: We do have strong references in mid-market HR services. I want to make sure we're being realistic here though. I'll get you those references, but I want to be transparent that procurement references and implementation timelines are interconnected issues. If we can build a longer implementation runway with your IT team, that might unlock some of these other constraints. Robert Martinez: Understood. But right now, based on what we're hearing, there are three significant barriers: the certification gap, the integration customization burden, and the ROI justification timeline. All three need to be addressable before my team can move this forward. David Patel: Agreed. And from my side, I need confidence that Type II is actually achievable and that you can scope the Workday work clearly so my team knows what we're committing to.
summary: David and Robert raised critical concerns about SOC 2 Type II certification requirements, Workday API integration complexity requiring 2-3 weeks of internal IT effort, and the need for demonstrated 18-month ROI to justify the 750k annual investment. Marcus acknowledged the legitimacy of all three barriers and offered to work with their security and legal teams on a compliance roadmap while discussing phased implementation and reference customer introductions.
objections_raised: Our SOC 2 certification is Type I, not Type II. We need Type II for our procurement to move forward.
Our current Workday integration does not support the custom payroll attributes that Lumos HR requires. We would need significant API work on our end.
At 750k ARR, this represents a 12 percent increase to our software spend. We need to see measurable ROI within the first 18 months to justify the investment to our board.
next_steps: Marcus to provide three vendor references from mid-market HR services companies with similar employee headcount and ARR range
Marcus to coordinate with Lumos professional services to document specific Workday API customization scope and estimated effort for David's team
Marcus to connect with Lumos security and legal teams to discuss Type II certification timeline and potential interim compliance documentation options
Follow-up call scheduled for May 5 to review reference checks and finalized integration scope document

title: Deal Review — Talent Compass Solutions Security Concerns
transcript: Marcus (AE): We need to talk about the security call this morning. Things took a turn. Jennifer (Manager): What happened? Marcus (AE): They came with a list of security requirements that weren't on our radar before. Their Chief Information Security Officer was very concerned about our data residency policies and whether we can meet their specific encryption standards. Sarah (SE): I was on the call too. The tone shifted pretty quickly from the demo where they were engaged and positive. They started asking pointed questions about our audit processes and vendor risk assessments. Marcus (AE): Exactly. The CISO basically said they need to run their own penetration testing before proceeding, which could add two to three months to their evaluation timeline. Jennifer (Manager): That's not uncommon for an HR platform, especially at their size. How concerned did they seem? Sarah (SE): Genuinely concerned. They mentioned they had a security incident with a previous vendor and are now much more stringent. I think this is legitimate caution rather than a deal killer, but they're definitely worried about risk. Marcus (AE): The good news is they didn't say no. They want a detailed security questionnaire completed, our latest audit reports, and proof of our penetration testing results. Jennifer (Manager): Okay, so we went from neutral after the demo to concerned now. We need to treat this seriously. Sarah (SE): I can coordinate with our security team to expedite the questionnaire responses and pull together all our compliance documentation. Marcus (AE): I should also reach out to their procurement team. Sometimes the CISO's concerns get addressed through the vendor risk management process, and we can parallelize that work. Jennifer (Manager): Good thinking. This is still a winnable deal, but we need to demonstrate we take security as seriously as they do. Let's not lose momentum here.
summary: Post-security review deal status shifted from neutral to concerned following stringent CISO requirements around data residency, encryption standards, and penetration testing. While not a deal killer, the extended evaluation timeline and risk assessment process now pose timeline risks. Team to mobilize security documentation and vendor risk management efforts.
action_items: Sarah to coordinate with security team to complete detailed security questionnaire within 5 business days
Compile latest SOC 2 audit reports, penetration testing results, and compliance certifications for delivery to Talent Compass
Marcus to engage with Talent Compass procurement team to parallel vendor risk management process alongside CISO technical review

note_preview: Security and compliance review call held with David Patel today
author: Marcus Chen
content: Had a security and compliance review call with David Patel today. He flagged that our SOC 2 Type I certification is not sufficient for their procurement requirements - they need Type II attestation to move forward. This is a hard blocker unless we can get certified or provide a workaround in the next 30 days.

note_preview: Objection - SOC 2 Type I certification insufficient, requires Type II
author: Marcus Chen
content: David Patel flagged a hard stop on security compliance during the review call - their procurement policy requires SOC 2 Type II certification and we only have Type I. This is a material gap that will block their board approval. We need to understand if this is a hard requirement or if there's any flexibility on their end before we proceed further.

subject: ROI Analysis and Financial Impact Assessment
body: Robert, following our security review with David this week, I wanted to address the investment question from your perspective as CFO. I understand the 750k ARR commitment represents a meaningful software spend increase, and your board will rightfully expect clear ROI metrics within 18 months. Let me break down the financial impact we discussed during discovery. Based on your current payroll processing costs and the manual work Jennifer's team handles monthly, we conservatively project 280 hours of annual labor recovery alone. At your blended HR staff rate, that's roughly 120k in direct savings. Additional benefits include reduced compliance risk exposure and the operational efficiency gains from eliminating dual-system reconciliation. I've attached a detailed ROI model that accounts for implementation costs and assumes a realistic 120-day ramp to full productivity. I want to make sure these numbers align with your expectations before we move forward. Could we schedule a brief call this week to walk through the assumptions together? Jennifer mentioned there are specific board metrics you focus on, and I'd like to ensure we're modeling the right outcomes. Best, Marcus

note_preview: Budget concern logged - 750k ARR requires 18-month ROI proof to board
author: Marcus Chen
content: Robert Martinez pushed back hard on the 750k ARR price tag today - he needs to show his board an 18-month ROI payback to justify the software spend increase. This is a real blocker unless we can quantify tangible savings from the payroll automation and compliance risk reduction. Need to work with Sarah on building a financial model that speaks to their specific pain points or this deal stalls in budget review.

subject: Re: ROI Analysis and Financial Impact Assessment
body: Marcus, I appreciate you taking the time to model out the financial impact. I have reviewed your ROI analysis and the 280 hours of annual labor recovery is a reasonable projection based on our current state process mapping. However, I need to flag some concerns before I can present this to our board with confidence. First, your 120-day ramp assumes our IT infrastructure can support the integration without significant custom API work on our end. David has flagged that our Workday setup will require substantial configuration effort that may extend timeline and create hidden costs we have not budgeted for. Second, the 750k annual commitment represents a 12 percent increase to our software portfolio spend. Our board approval threshold requires demonstrated ROI within 18 months, not projections. Given the technical uncertainty David raised around our custom payroll attributes, I am concerned about the implementation risk to your timeline assumptions. Can we reconvene with David and Jennifer to walk through the technical integration requirements before I move this forward financially? I want to understand the true scope of work on both sides. Robert

note_preview: Risk flag - multiple unresolved objections threatening deal progression
author: Marcus Chen
content: We have at least three critical objections sitting unresolved and they're all blocking forward movement. David is holding hard on the Workday API integration work required on their end, Robert needs iron-clad ROI proof for his board before approving the budget, and the SOC 2 Type II gap is a procurement showstopper that we can't just talk around. If we don't get at least two of these resolved in the next week, this deal is going to slip into negotiation in a weakened position.

note_preview: Timeline slippage risk - 90 day implementation too aggressive for IT capacity
author: Marcus Chen
content: David Patel pushed back hard on the 90-day implementation timeline during our call today. He made it clear his team is already maxed out with the ERP migration and cannot commit resources to parallel track this deployment. This is a hard constraint we need to address in renegotiations or we risk losing the deal entirely.

subject: Implementation Timeline Concerns and Resource Constraints
body: David, I wanted to circle back on the implementation timeline concern you raised during our last sync. I completely understand that your team is stretched thin with the ERP migration, and a 90-day sprint isn't realistic given those constraints. Rather than push an aggressive timeline that sets us both up for failure, I'd like to propose we explore a phased approach. We could front-load the core payroll integration work during months one and two when you have some breathing room post-ERP stabilization, then move the custom leave accrual policies and reporting features into months three and four. This gives your team capacity to properly handle the Workday API work without compromising the quality of implementation. I know Jennifer and Robert are both invested in getting this right, and frankly, a slower rollout with clean execution will demonstrate better ROI to your board than a rushed deployment. Can we schedule a brief call this week to map out what a 120-140 day timeline would look like with your IT priorities in mind? I want to make sure we're setting realistic expectations before we move to final contract terms. Thanks for your candor on this, David. It's exactly the kind of partnership feedback that helps us succeed together.

subject: Re: Implementation Timeline Concerns and Resource Constraints
body:
Marcus, Sarah - I appreciate you reaching out on the timeline, but I need to be direct about where we stand operationally. Our team is currently deep into the ERP migration with Workday, and committing to a 90-day implementation for Lumos HR isn't realistic given our current capacity constraints. We're already stretched across three major initiatives.

Beyond timeline, we still have unresolved technical questions around the custom payroll attributes integration that your platform currently doesn't support. That API work would fall on us, which compounds the resource problem.

I want this to work, but I need to see either a 120-day+ implementation window or a revised scope that doesn't require custom Workday modifications on our end. Can we schedule a call to explore whether those adjustments are possible? Otherwise, we may need to table this until Q4 when our ERP stabilizes.

Let me know your thoughts.

channel.name: deal-talent-compass-closeout
channel.topic: Talent Compass - Final negotiation and outcome

messages.body: Pre-call notes for tomorrow with Talent Compass. David wants to know if we can guarantee 120 days for implementation. 90 is dead. Also they still don't have the references they need and procurement is pushing back.
Can we do 120? What does Sarah say on resource side?
We can do it but it means staggering the Workday API phase. That gives their IT team breathing room. Issue is we are eating implementation margin if we absorb most of the API work.
Did Derek get anywhere with the references? Because if we can't get three solid refs, Robert is not taking this to his board regardless of timeline or ROI promises.
Two confirmed. Third one is still iffy. Customer is willing but their IT director is not comfortable being a reference for a product they only went live on four months ago.

Call wrapped. David is okay with 120 days if we phase the Workday work. Robert needs confirmation on ROI from our customer success team before he touches this again. And they are still one reference short.
Can Derek set up an ROI call? We need to show them real numbers on time saved and compliance risk reduction.

I can do that but to be honest their leave management workload is not high enough to justify 750k. We are looking at maybe 400k in annual savings across all the manual leave processing. That is not an 18-month ROI story.
That is the problem. 400k in savings does not hit their 18-month breakeven target. And we still cannot get a third reference. I think this deal is slipping.

Let me know what Robert says when he sees the numbers. If those 400k savings are real they might have to accept the longer payoff. But without the third reference and a weaker ROI story, I agree it is getting risky.

Robert just sent a message. They are not moving forward. Board rejected it based on ROI timeline and lack of the three vendor references. Offered a few more discounts and a Type II cert roadmap but he said procurement has already said no.
Tough one. Document the objections and we will revisit in 18 months when they actually need the functionality and their IT team has bandwidth. Move to closed lost.

title: Contract Terms and Implementation Timeline Discussion
transcript:
Marcus Chen: Good morning everyone, thanks for taking the time today. I know we have a lot to unpack around contract terms and the implementation timeline. I want to make sure we address David's concerns head-on and find a path forward that works for your IT team. Jennifer, Robert, David - where would you like to start?

Jennifer Walsh: Marcus, I appreciate the directness. Look, I remain excited about what Lumos HR can do for our organization. But we need to resolve some fundamental issues before I can champion this internally. David, you've been the most vocal about the technical constraints.

David Patel: Thanks Jennifer. Marcus, the 90-day implementation timeline is simply not viable. Our team is mid-cycle with the ERP migration, and we have two critical infrastructure projects scheduled. Adding a third major integration work stream would compromise all three initiatives. We need a minimum of 120 days, ideally 150 days for proper testing and training.

Marcus Chen: I hear you, David. We've built flexibility into our implementation methodology. What if we staggered the rollout - core payroll and benefits in the initial 90 days, with the custom leave accrual policies and reporting enhancements phased in over months two and three? That spreads the resource burden and aligns with your project calendar.

David Patel: That helps, but we still have the Workday integration issue. Your system requires API connectivity that our current Workday instance doesn't natively support. We explored this with your engineering team. The custom payroll attributes you need would require significant development work on our side. Who bears that cost?

Robert Martinez: That's exactly the conversation I need to have, David. Marcus, we've done the financial analysis. A 750,000 dollar annual commitment represents a 12 percent increase to our total software spend. The ROI model needs to show concrete, measurable improvements within 18 months - not projections. Our board will ask hard questions about this investment. And frankly, if we're also absorbing API development costs, the economics become very difficult.

Marcus Chen: Robert, I understand. Let me be transparent here. On the API customization, we typically absorb those costs for enterprise deals in your range. We can offer a 15 percent discount on year-one fees and extend our success guarantee to include specific process metrics - reduction in payroll cycle time, improved compliance reporting accuracy. That puts real financial skin in the game for us.

Jennifer Walsh: That's movement, Marcus, and I appreciate it. But Robert's right - we need clarity on what success looks like. And frankly, the security conversation David raised earlier is still unresolved. Your SOC 2 Type I certification doesn't meet our procurement requirements. Our insurance broker specifically flagged that.

David Patel: Jennifer's correct. Type I is a point-in-time audit. Type II requires ongoing monitoring for a minimum of six months. We can't move forward without Type II. That's a hard requirement from our security and legal teams.

Marcus Chen: That's fair. We're actually on track for Type II certification in Q3 2026, about four months out. What if we structure the contract with a provision that allows you to pause go-live by 60 days pending successful Type II audit completion? We'd cover the delay costs. Would that address the concern?

Robert Martinez: That's creative, but it adds contingency risk. And we still haven't resolved the vendor reference issue. Procurement needs three references from companies with at least 1000 employees in similar industries. Our team has been unable to reach customers who fit that criteria.

Marcus Chen: I can provide two solid references who meet those criteria - both are in the 1200-employee range and in HR services. For a third, we'd need a week or two, but I'm confident we can source that. Can we move forward with that commitment?

Jennifer Walsh: Marcus, I want this to work. But we're stacking contingencies on top of unresolved technical requirements. We need to see a clear, executable path. Can you and Sarah come back with a revised implementation plan, the reference list, and a formal amendment addressing the Type II certification timeline?
summary: The team discussed critical blockers preventing contract execution: David Patel's aggressive 90-day implementation timeline conflicting with IT capacity and requiring 120-150 days minimum, unresolved Workday API integration costs, Robert Martinez's ROI concerns requiring 18-month measurable returns, and security certification gaps with only Type I SOC 2 available instead of required Type II. Marcus offered staggered rollout, cost-sharing on API work, discount incentives, and Type II certification contingencies, but multiple unresolved issues remain.
objections_raised: 90-day implementation timeline is too aggressive given ERP migration and resource constraints
Workday API integration requires custom payroll attribute work and cost allocation is unclear
750k ARR investment requires 18-month measurable ROI for board approval
SOC 2 Type I certification insufficient, Type II required by procurement and legal
Unable to obtain three vendor references from similarly-sized companies
next_steps: Marcus to provide revised implementation plan with staggered 90-150 day phased approach
Marcus to submit list of two qualified vendor references within one week and identify third reference
Marcus to provide formal contract amendment addressing Type II SOC 2 certification contingency and go-live delay provisions
Marcus and Sarah to schedule follow-up meeting after documentation is submitted

title: Close Plan Session — Talent Compass Solutions Deal Replan
transcript: Jennifer (Manager): Marcus, I reviewed the notes from the contract negotiation call. This doesn't look good. Marcus (AE): No, it's not. They came back with major revisions to the implementation timeline and some hard requirements we can't accommodate. Jennifer (Manager): Walk me through it. Marcus (AE): They want a six-month implementation period instead of our standard four months. They're also demanding significant liability caps and indemnification language that our legal team says would expose us. Sarah (SE): The conversation went downhill pretty fast. Their legal team was adversarial from the start, and I could tell the internal stakeholders were frustrated. They kept referencing the security concerns from April and saying they need ironclad protections. Jennifer (Manager): So the security issues from the previous call created a domino effect. Marcus (AE): Exactly. What started as a concerned buyer in April turned into a negative, almost hostile tone today. They're also asking for price reductions because of the extended implementation and support requirements. Sarah (SE): I think they lost confidence in us somewhere along the way. Maybe we didn't address their security concerns thoroughly enough, or maybe they found an alternative solution. Jennifer (Manager): Can we salvage this? Marcus (AE): I'm not sure. The requirements they're asking for would fundamentally change our delivery model and put us at significant financial and legal risk on a 750k deal. Jennifer (Manager): So we're facing a critical decision point. Do we walk away or do we try one more conversation to find middle ground? Sarah (SE): I think we need to be honest with ourselves. If they're this adversarial on contract terms, the implementation will be painful. Jennifer (Manager): Agreed. Marcus, I want you to schedule a final check-in call with their executive sponsor next week. Lay out our position clearly. If they won't budge on the key issues, we recommend both parties move on professionally. Marcus (AE): I can do that, but I'm preparing for a no-go outcome. This deal has shifted from on-track to stalled, and I don't see a realistic path to close given their demands.
summary: Critical deal replan session following major sentiment deterioration from concerned to negative during contract negotiation. Buyer now demanding unrealistic implementation timelines, liability terms, and pricing adjustments. Team to conduct final executive sponsor conversation to determine go-no-go decision.
action_items: Marcus to schedule executive sponsor call within 7 days to present clear position on non-negotiable contract terms and implementation requirements
Prepare summary document outlining where buyer demands exceed risk tolerance and product delivery capabilities
Plan deal closure communication and documentation in case buyer relationship ends

note_preview: Contract terms and implementation timeline call held, no resolution achieved
author: Marcus Chen
content: Call with Jennifer, Robert, and David today went nowhere on timeline and contract terms. They remain firm on needing a 120-day implementation minimum due to IT capacity constraints from their ERP migration, and David is still holding on the SOC 2 Type II certification requirement before legal will approve. No movement on either front and we're running out of time before their board review deadline.

subject: Vendor Reference Requirements - Procurement Roadblock
body: Robert, I wanted to follow up on a critical blocker that came up during our contract discussion last week. Your procurement team has requested three vendor references from companies in the 1000+ employee range within the HR Tech space. I understand this is a standard requirement for deals at this investment level, and we take it seriously. Unfortunately, we've hit a wall here. Our current customer base in that specific segment is limited, and we're unable to provide the three references your team needs to move forward. I wanted to be direct about this rather than delay further. We have a few options to explore: we could provide references from similar-sized companies outside HR Tech, we could connect your team directly with our customer success leader to discuss our implementation track record, or we could explore alternative procurement paths that your team might find acceptable. Given the other concerns we've discussed around the SOC 2 Type II certification and implementation timeline, I want to make sure we're solving for procurement in a way that actually works for Talent Compass. Can we schedule a brief call with you and Jennifer to discuss viable alternatives?

note_preview: Procurement roadblock - unable to obtain three vendor references for similar companies
author: Marcus Chen
content: Robert Martinez came back with a hard no on the vendor reference requirement - their procurement team needs three references from companies with 1000+ employees in similar industries and we simply don't have that list depth. This is a deal killer unless we can somehow manufacture those references or get an exception, which feels impossible at this point given how rigid their procurement process is.

subject: Re: Vendor Reference Requirements - Procurement Roadblock
body: Marcus, I appreciate you reaching out, but I need to be direct about where we stand. Our procurement team has made multiple attempts to secure the three vendor references you requested from companies in our size and industry vertical - 1000+ employees in HR services. Unfortunately, we have not been able to obtain satisfactory references that meet those criteria. This is a hard requirement for our board approval, not something we can work around. Combined with the SOC 2 Type II certification gap and the aggressive 90-day implementation timeline that conflicts with our ERP migration, we're looking at significant risk on multiple fronts. I want this to work - Jennifer and I both see the value in what Lumos HR brings to the table. But right now, the procurement hurdle is the immediate blocker. Can you provide us with a realistic path forward on the reference requirement, or do we need to acknowledge this may not be the right timing for Talent Compass?

note_preview: Deal lost - procurement requirements unmet, security certification gap
author: Marcus Chen
content: Lost this deal on May 27th. Talent Compass couldn't get procurement to sign off because we don't have SOC 2 Type II certification and they couldn't obtain three vendor references from companies over 1000 employees in their industry. The combination of the security gap, David Patel's integration concerns, Robert Martinez's ROI requirements, and the aggressive 90-day timeline proved to be an insurmountable wall even with Jennifer as our champion.
